title: Asteroid

infobox.value: is a [minor planet](/wiki/Minor_planet "Minor planet")—an object larger than a [meteoroid](/wiki/Meteoroid "Meteoroid") (thus 1 meter or larger) that is neither a [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet") nor an identified [comet](/wiki/Comet "Comet")—that orbits within the [inner Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System#Inner_Solar_System "Solar System") or is co-orbital with [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter") ([Trojan asteroids](/wiki/Trojan_asteroid "Trojan asteroid")). Asteroids are rocky, metallic, or icy bodies with no atmosphere, and are broadly classified into [C-type](/wiki/C-type_asteroid "C-type asteroid") ([carbonaceous](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon")), [M-type](/wiki/M-type_asteroid "M-type asteroid") ([metallic](/wiki/Metal "Metal")), or [S-type](/wiki/S-type_asteroid "S-type asteroid") ([silicaceous](/wiki/Silica "Silica"))[[1]](#cite_note-DeMeoCarry2013-1). The size and shape of asteroids vary significantly, ranging from small rubble piles under a kilometer across to [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)"), a [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet") almost 1000 km in diameter. A body is classified as a [comet](/wiki/Comet "Comet"), not an asteroid, if it shows a coma (tail) when warmed by solar radiation, although recent observations suggest a continuum between these types of bodies.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Minor planets found within the inner Solar System

This article is about the type of celestial bodies. For other uses, see [Asteroid (disambiguation)](/wiki/Asteroid_(disambiguation) "Asteroid (disambiguation)"). Not to be confused with [Astroid](/wiki/Astroid "Astroid").

[![433 Eros photographed by NEAR Shoemaker](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Eros_-_PIA02923_%28color%29.jpg/120px-Eros_-_PIA02923_%28color%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eros_-_PIA02923_(color).jpg)

[![Galileo image of 243 Ida (the dot to the right is its moon Dactyl)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/243_ida_crop.jpg/250px-243_ida_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:243_ida_crop.jpg)

[![Dawn image of the dwarf planet Ceres.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Ceres_-_RC3_-_Haulani_Crater_%2822381131691%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Ceres_-_RC3_-_Haulani_Crater_%2822381131691%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceres_-_RC3_-_Haulani_Crater_(22381131691)_(cropped).jpg)

[![OSIRIS-REx image of 101955 Bennu, a rubble-pile asteroid](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Bennu_mosaic_OSIRIS-REx_%28square%29.png/250px-Bennu_mosaic_OSIRIS-REx_%28square%29.png)](/wiki/File:Bennu_mosaic_OSIRIS-REx_(square).png)

Images of [visited asteroids](/wiki/List_of_minor_planets_and_comets_visited_by_spacecraft "List of minor planets and comets visited by spacecraft") illustrating their differences: (top row) [433 Eros](/wiki/433_Eros "433 Eros") and [243 Ida](/wiki/243_Ida "243 Ida") with its moon [Dactyl](/wiki/Dactyl_(moon) "Dactyl (moon)"), (bottom row) [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)") and [101955 Bennu](/wiki/101955_Bennu "101955 Bennu").
*Sizes are not to scale.*

An **asteroid** is a [minor planet](/wiki/Minor_planet "Minor planet")—an object larger than a [meteoroid](/wiki/Meteoroid "Meteoroid") (thus 1 meter or larger) that is neither a [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet") nor an identified [comet](/wiki/Comet "Comet")—that orbits within the [inner Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System#Inner_Solar_System "Solar System") or is co-orbital with [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter") ([Trojan asteroids](/wiki/Trojan_asteroid "Trojan asteroid")). Asteroids are rocky, metallic, or icy bodies with no atmosphere, and are broadly classified into [C-type](/wiki/C-type_asteroid "C-type asteroid") ([carbonaceous](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon")), [M-type](/wiki/M-type_asteroid "M-type asteroid") ([metallic](/wiki/Metal "Metal")), or [S-type](/wiki/S-type_asteroid "S-type asteroid") ([silicaceous](/wiki/Silica "Silica"))[[1]](#cite_note-DeMeoCarry2013-1). The size and shape of asteroids vary significantly, ranging from small rubble piles under a kilometer across to [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)"), a [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet") almost 1000 km in diameter. A body is classified as a [comet](/wiki/Comet "Comet"), not an asteroid, if it shows a coma (tail) when warmed by solar radiation, although recent observations suggest a continuum between these types of bodies.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

Of the roughly one million known asteroids,[[4]](#cite_note-4) the greatest number are located between the orbits of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") and [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), approximately 2 to 4 [astronomical units](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit") (AU) from the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), in a region known as the main [asteroid belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt"). The total mass of all the asteroids combined is only 3% that of [Earth's Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"). The majority of main belt asteroids follow slightly elliptical, stable orbits, revolving in the same direction as the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") and taking from three to six years to complete a full circuit of the Sun.[[5]](#cite_note-press-kit-5)

Asteroids have been observed from Earth since their discovery in 1801. The first close-up observation of an asteroid was made by the [Galileo spacecraft](/wiki/Galileo_(spacecraft) "Galileo (spacecraft)"). Several dedicated missions to asteroids were subsequently launched by [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") and [JAXA](/wiki/JAXA "JAXA"), with plans for other missions in progress. NASA's *[NEAR Shoemaker](/wiki/NEAR_Shoemaker "NEAR Shoemaker")* studied [Eros](/wiki/433_Eros "433 Eros"), and *[Dawn](/wiki/Dawn_(spacecraft) "Dawn (spacecraft)")* observed [Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta") and [Ceres](/wiki/1_Ceres "1 Ceres"). JAXA's missions *[Hayabusa](/wiki/Hayabusa "Hayabusa")* and *[Hayabusa2](/wiki/Hayabusa2 "Hayabusa2")* studied and returned samples of [Itokawa](/wiki/25143_Itokawa "25143 Itokawa") and [Ryugu](/wiki/162173_Ryugu "162173 Ryugu"), respectively. [OSIRIS-REx](/wiki/OSIRIS-REx "OSIRIS-REx") studied [Bennu](/wiki/101955_Bennu "101955 Bennu"), collecting a sample in 2020 which was delivered back to Earth in 2023. NASA's *[Lucy](/wiki/Lucy_(spacecraft) "Lucy (spacecraft)")*, launched in 2021, is tasked with studying ten different asteroids, two from the [main belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt") and eight [Jupiter trojans](/wiki/Jupiter_trojan "Jupiter trojan"). NASA's [Double Asteroid Redirection Test](/wiki/Double_Asteroid_Redirection_Test "Double Asteroid Redirection Test") (DART), launched in 2021, was intentionally crashed into the non-threatening asteroid [Dimorphos](/wiki/Dimorphos "Dimorphos") in September 2022 as a test of [planetary defense](/wiki/Asteroid_impact_avoidance "Asteroid impact avoidance") technology. NASA's *[Psyche](/wiki/Psyche_(spacecraft) "Psyche (spacecraft)")*, launched October 2023, aims to study the metallic [asteroid Psyche](/wiki/16_Psyche "16 Psyche"). ESA's *[Hera](/wiki/Hera_(space_mission) "Hera (space mission)")*, launched in October 2024, is intended to study the results of the DART impact. CNSA's *[Tianwen-2](/wiki/Tianwen-2 "Tianwen-2")* was launched in May 2025,[[6]](#cite_note-auto-6) to explore the [co-orbital](/wiki/Co-orbital "Co-orbital") near-Earth asteroid [469219 Kamoʻoalewa](/wiki/469219_Kamo%CA%BBoalewa "469219 Kamoʻoalewa") and the [active asteroid](/wiki/Active_asteroid "Active asteroid") [311P/PanSTARRS](/wiki/311P/PanSTARRS "311P/PanSTARRS") and collecting samples of the regolith of Kamo'oalewa.[[7]](#cite_note-nature20190430-7)

Near-Earth asteroids have the potential for catastrophic consequences if they strike Earth, with a notable example being the [Chicxulub impact](/wiki/Chicxulub_impact "Chicxulub impact"), widely thought to have induced the [Cretaceous–Paleogene mass extinction](/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event "Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event").

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Asteroidsscale.jpg/250px-Asteroidsscale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Asteroidsscale.jpg)

A composite image, to the same scale, of the asteroids imaged at high resolution prior to 2012. They are, from largest to smallest: [4 Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta"), [21 Lutetia](/wiki/21_Lutetia "21 Lutetia"), [253 Mathilde](/wiki/253_Mathilde "253 Mathilde"), [243 Ida](/wiki/243_Ida "243 Ida") and its moon [Dactyl](/wiki/Dactyl_(asteroid) "Dactyl (asteroid)"), [433 Eros](/wiki/433_Eros "433 Eros"), [951 Gaspra](/wiki/951_Gaspra "951 Gaspra"), [2867 Šteins](/wiki/2867_%C5%A0teins "2867 Šteins"), [25143 Itokawa](/wiki/25143_Itokawa "25143 Itokawa").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Ceres_and_Vesta%2C_Moon_size_comparison.jpg/250px-Ceres_and_Vesta%2C_Moon_size_comparison.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ceres_and_Vesta,_Moon_size_comparison.jpg)

[Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta") (left), with [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)") (center) and the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") (right) shown to scale

In 2006, the [International Astronomical Union](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union") (IAU) introduced the currently preferred broad term [small Solar System body](/wiki/Small_Solar_System_bodies "Small Solar System bodies"), defined as an object in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") that is neither a [planet](/wiki/Planet "Planet"), a [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet"), nor a [natural satellite](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite"); this includes asteroids, comets, and more recently discovered classes.[[8]](#cite_note-8) According to IAU, "the term 'minor planet' may still be used, but generally, 'Small Solar System Body' will be preferred."[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Historically, the first discovered asteroid, [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)"), was at first considered a new planet.[[a]](#cite_note-10) It was followed by the discovery of other similar bodies, which with the equipment of the time appeared to be points of light like stars, showing little or no planetary disc, though readily distinguishable from stars due to their apparent motions. This prompted the astronomer Sir [William Herschel](/wiki/William_Herschel "William Herschel") to propose the term *asteroid*,[[b]](#cite_note-14) coined in Greek as ἀστεροειδής, or *asteroeidēs*, meaning 'star-like, star-shaped', and derived from the Ancient Greek [ἀστήρ](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%80%CF%83%CF%84%CE%AE%CF%81 "wikt:ἀστήρ") *astēr* 'star, planet'. In the early second half of the 19th century, the terms *asteroid* and *planet* (not always qualified as "minor") were still used interchangeably.[[c]](#cite_note-16)

Traditionally, small bodies orbiting the Sun were classified as [comets](/wiki/Comet "Comet"), asteroids, or [meteoroids](/wiki/Meteoroid "Meteoroid"), with anything smaller than one meter across being called a meteoroid. The term *asteroid,* never officially defined,[[14]](#cite_note-17) can be informally used to mean "an irregularly shaped rocky body orbiting the Sun that does not qualify as a planet or a dwarf planet under the IAU definitions".[[15]](#cite_note-18) The main difference between an asteroid and a comet is that a comet shows a coma (tail) due to [sublimation](/wiki/Outgassing "Outgassing") of its near-surface ices by solar radiation. A few objects were first classified as minor planets but later showed evidence of cometary activity. Conversely, some (perhaps all) comets are eventually depleted of their surface [volatile ices](/wiki/Volatile_(astrogeology) "Volatile (astrogeology)") and become asteroid-like. A further distinction is that comets typically have more eccentric orbits than most asteroids; highly eccentric asteroids are probably dormant or extinct comets.[[16]](#cite_note-19)

The minor planets beyond Jupiter's orbit are sometimes also called "asteroids", especially in popular presentations.[[d]](#cite_note-21) However, it is becoming increasingly common for the term *asteroid* to be restricted to minor planets of the inner Solar System.[[18]](#cite_note-KBOasteroids-22) Therefore, this article will restrict itself for the most part to the classical asteroids: objects of the [asteroid belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt"), [Jupiter trojans](/wiki/Jupiter_trojan "Jupiter trojan"), and [near-Earth objects](/wiki/Near-Earth_object "Near-Earth object").

For almost two centuries after the discovery of [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)") in 1801, all known asteroids spent most of their time at or within the orbit of Jupiter, though a few, such as [944 Hidalgo](/wiki/944_Hidalgo "944 Hidalgo"), ventured farther for part of their orbit. Starting in 1977 with [2060 Chiron](/wiki/2060_Chiron "2060 Chiron"), astronomers discovered small bodies that permanently resided further out than Jupiter, now called [centaurs](/wiki/Centaur_(minor_planet) "Centaur (minor planet)"). In 1992, [15760 Albion](/wiki/15760_Albion "15760 Albion") was discovered, the first object beyond the orbit of [Neptune](/wiki/Neptune "Neptune") (other than [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto "Pluto")); soon large numbers of similar objects were observed, now called [trans-Neptunian object](/wiki/Trans-Neptunian_object "Trans-Neptunian object"). Further out are [Kuiper-belt objects](/wiki/Kuiper_belt "Kuiper belt"), [scattered-disc objects](/wiki/Scattered-disc_object "Scattered-disc object"), and the much more distant [Oort cloud](/wiki/Oort_cloud "Oort cloud"), hypothesized to be the main reservoir of dormant comets. They inhabit the cold outer reaches of the Solar System where ices remain solid and comet-like bodies exhibit little cometary activity; if centaurs or trans-Neptunian objects were to venture close to the Sun, their volatile ices would [sublimate](/wiki/Sublimation_(phase_transition) "Sublimation (phase transition)"), and traditional approaches would classify them as comets.

The [Kuiper-belt](/wiki/Kuiper_belt "Kuiper belt") bodies are called "objects" partly to avoid the need to classify them as asteroids or comets.[[18]](#cite_note-KBOasteroids-22) They are thought to be predominantly comet-like in composition, though some may be more akin to asteroids.[[19]](#cite_note-23) Most do not have the highly eccentric orbits associated with comets, and the ones so far discovered are larger than traditional [comet nuclei](/wiki/Comet_nucleus "Comet nucleus"). Other recent observations, such as the analysis of the cometary dust collected by the [*Stardust*](/wiki/Stardust_(spacecraft) "Stardust (spacecraft)") probe, are increasingly blurring the distinction between comets and asteroids,[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2) suggesting "a continuum between asteroids and comets" rather than a sharp dividing line.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

In 2006, the IAU created the class of [dwarf planets](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet") for the largest minor planets—those massive enough to have become ellipsoidal under their own gravity. Only the largest object in the asteroid belt has been placed in this category: [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)"), at about 975 km (606 mi) across.[[20]](#cite_note-dwarf1-24)[[21]](#cite_note-dwarf2-25)
Asteroids have been distinguished from the concept of [planetoid](/wiki/Planetoid "Planetoid") by describing them respectively as [undifferentiated and differentiated](/wiki/Planetary_differentiation "Planetary differentiation"), like dwarf planets.[[22]](#cite_note-f957-26)

## History of observations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History of observations")]

Despite their large numbers, asteroids are a relatively recent discovery, with the first one—Ceres—only being identified in 1801.[[23]](#cite_note-cunningham2001-27) Only one asteroid, [4 Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta"), which has a relatively [reflective surface](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo"), is normally visible to the naked eye in dark skies when it is favorably positioned. Rarely, small asteroids passing close to Earth may be briefly visible to the naked eye.[[24]](#cite_note-SPACE-2004-02-04-28) As of May 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit), the [Minor Planet Center](/wiki/Minor_Planet_Center "Minor Planet Center") had data on 1,460,356 minor planets in the inner and outer Solar System, of which about 826,864 had enough information to be given numbered designations.[[25]](#cite_note-MPCcount-29)

### Discovery of Ceres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Discovery of Ceres")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Piazzi.jpg/250px-Piazzi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Piazzi.jpg)

[Giuseppe Velasquez](/wiki/Giuseppe_Velasquez "Giuseppe Velasquez"), *Giuseppe Piazzi and Ceres*, oil on canvas, c. 1803

In 1772, German astronomer [Johann Elert Bode](/wiki/Johann_Elert_Bode "Johann Elert Bode"), citing [Johann Daniel Titius](/wiki/Johann_Daniel_Titius "Johann Daniel Titius"), published a numerical procession known as the [Titius–Bode law](/wiki/Titius%E2%80%93Bode_law "Titius–Bode law") (now discredited). Except for an unexplained gap between Mars and Jupiter, Bode's formula seemed to predict the orbits of the known planets.[[26]](#cite_note-hoskin-30)[[27]](#cite_note-Hogg1948-31) He wrote the following explanation for the existence of a "missing planet":

> This latter point seems in particular to follow from the astonishing relation which the known six planets observe in their distances from the Sun. Let the distance from the Sun to Saturn be taken as 100, then Mercury is separated by 4 such parts from the Sun. Venus is 4 + 3 = 7. The Earth 4 + 6 = 10. Mars 4 + 12 = 16. Now comes a gap in this so orderly progression. After Mars there follows a space of 4 + 24 = 28 parts, in which no planet has yet been seen. Can one believe that the Founder of the universe had left this space empty? Certainly not. From here we come to the distance of Jupiter by 4 + 48 = 52 parts, and finally to that of Saturn by 4 + 96 = 100 parts.[[28]](#cite_note-discovery-32)

Bode's formula predicted another planet would be found with an orbital radius near 2.8 [astronomical units](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit") (AU), or 420 million km, from the Sun.[[27]](#cite_note-Hogg1948-31) The Titius–Bode law got a boost with [William Herschel](/wiki/William_Herschel "William Herschel")'s discovery of [Uranus](/wiki/Uranus "Uranus") near the predicted distance for a planet beyond [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn").[[26]](#cite_note-hoskin-30) In 1800, a group headed by [Franz Xaver von Zach](/wiki/Franz_Xaver_von_Zach "Franz Xaver von Zach"), editor of the German astronomical journal *Monatliche Correspondenz* (Monthly Correspondence), sent requests to 24 experienced astronomers (whom he dubbed the "[celestial police](/wiki/Celestial_police "Celestial police")"),[[27]](#cite_note-Hogg1948-31) asking that they combine their efforts and begin a methodical search for the expected planet.[[27]](#cite_note-Hogg1948-31) Although they did not discover Ceres, they later found the asteroids [2 Pallas](/wiki/2_Pallas "2 Pallas"), [3 Juno](/wiki/3_Juno "3 Juno") and [4 Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta").[[27]](#cite_note-Hogg1948-31)

One of the astronomers selected for the search was [Giuseppe Piazzi](/wiki/Giuseppe_Piazzi "Giuseppe Piazzi"), a Catholic [priest](/wiki/Priest "Priest") at the Academy of Palermo, Sicily. Before receiving his invitation to join the group, Piazzi discovered Ceres on 1 January 1801.[[29]](#cite_note-NASA-20160126-33) He was searching for "the 87th [star] of the Catalogue of the Zodiacal stars of [Mr la Caille](/wiki/Nicolas_Louis_de_Lacaille "Nicolas Louis de Lacaille")",[[26]](#cite_note-hoskin-30) but found that "it was preceded by another".[[26]](#cite_note-hoskin-30) Instead of a star, Piazzi had found a moving star-like object, which he first thought was a comet:[[30]](#cite_note-Forbes1971-34)

> The light was a little faint, and of the colour of [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), but similar to many others which generally are reckoned of the eighth [magnitude](/wiki/Apparent_magnitude "Apparent magnitude"). Therefore I had no doubt of its being any other than a fixed star. [...] The evening of the third, my suspicion was converted into certainty, being assured it was not a fixed star. Nevertheless before I made it known, I waited till the evening of the fourth, when I had the satisfaction to see it had moved at the same rate as on the preceding days.[[26]](#cite_note-hoskin-30)

Piazzi observed Ceres a total of 24 times, the final time on 11 February 1801, when illness interrupted his work. He announced his discovery on 24 January 1801 in letters to only two fellow astronomers, his compatriot [Barnaba Oriani](/wiki/Barnaba_Oriani "Barnaba Oriani") of Milan and Bode in Berlin.[[23]](#cite_note-cunningham2001-27) He reported it as a comet but "since its movement is so slow and rather uniform, it has occurred to me several times that it might be something better than a comet".[[26]](#cite_note-hoskin-30) In April, Piazzi sent his complete observations to Oriani, Bode, and French astronomer [Jérôme Lalande](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_Lalande "Jérôme Lalande"). The information was published in the September 1801 issue of the *Monatliche Correspondenz*.[[30]](#cite_note-Forbes1971-34)

By this time, the apparent position of Ceres had changed (mostly due to Earth's motion around the Sun), and was too close to the Sun's glare for other astronomers to confirm Piazzi's observations. Toward the end of the year, Ceres should have been visible again, but after such a long time it was difficult to predict its exact position. To recover Ceres, mathematician [Carl Friedrich Gauss](/wiki/Carl_Friedrich_Gauss "Carl Friedrich Gauss"), then 24 years old, developed an [efficient method](/wiki/Gauss%27s_method "Gauss's method") of [orbit determination](/wiki/Orbit_determination "Orbit determination").[[30]](#cite_note-Forbes1971-34) In a few weeks, he predicted the path of Ceres and sent his results to von Zach. On 31 December 1801, von Zach and fellow celestial policeman [Heinrich W. M. Olbers](/wiki/Heinrich_Wilhelm_Matth%C3%A4us_Olbers "Heinrich Wilhelm Matthäus Olbers") found Ceres near the predicted position and thus recovered it.[[30]](#cite_note-Forbes1971-34) At 2.8 AU from the Sun, Ceres appeared to fit the Titius–Bode law almost perfectly; however, Neptune, once discovered in 1846, was 8 AU closer than predicted, leading most astronomers to conclude that the law was a coincidence.[[31]](#cite_note-35) Piazzi named the newly discovered object *Ceres Ferdinandea,* "in honor of the [patron goddess of Sicily](/wiki/Ceres_(Roman_mythology) "Ceres (Roman mythology)") and of [King Ferdinand of Bourbon](/wiki/Ferdinand_I_of_the_Two_Sicilies "Ferdinand I of the Two Sicilies")".[[28]](#cite_note-discovery-32)

### Further search

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Further search")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Moon_and_Asteroids_1_to_10.svg/250px-Moon_and_Asteroids_1_to_10.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Moon_and_Asteroids_1_to_10.svg)

Sizes of the first ten discovered asteroids, compared to the Moon

Three other asteroids ([2 Pallas](/wiki/2_Pallas "2 Pallas"), [3 Juno](/wiki/3_Juno "3 Juno"), and [4 Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta")) were discovered by von Zach's group over the next few years, with Vesta found in 1807.[[27]](#cite_note-Hogg1948-31) No new asteroids were discovered until 1845. Amateur astronomer [Karl Ludwig Hencke](/wiki/Karl_Ludwig_Hencke "Karl Ludwig Hencke") started his searches of new asteroids in 1830, and fifteen years later, while looking for Vesta, he found the asteroid later named [5 Astraea](/wiki/5_Astraea "5 Astraea"). It was the first new asteroid discovery in 38 years. [Carl Friedrich Gauss](/wiki/Carl_Friedrich_Gauss "Carl Friedrich Gauss") was given the honor of naming the asteroid. After this, other astronomers joined; 15 asteroids were found by the end of 1851. In 1868, when [James Craig Watson](/wiki/James_Craig_Watson "James Craig Watson") discovered the 100th asteroid, the [French Academy of Sciences](/wiki/French_Academy_of_Sciences "French Academy of Sciences") engraved the faces of [Karl Theodor Robert Luther](/wiki/Karl_Theodor_Robert_Luther "Karl Theodor Robert Luther"), [John Russell Hind](/wiki/John_Russell_Hind "John Russell Hind"), and [Hermann Goldschmidt](/wiki/Hermann_Mayer_Salomon_Goldschmidt "Hermann Mayer Salomon Goldschmidt"), the three most successful asteroid-hunters at that time, on a commemorative medallion marking the event.[[32]](#cite_note-dawn-community-36)

In 1891, [Max Wolf](/wiki/Maximilian_Franz_Joseph_Cornelius_Wolf "Maximilian Franz Joseph Cornelius Wolf") pioneered the use of [astrophotography](/wiki/Astrophotography "Astrophotography") to detect asteroids, which appeared as short streaks on long-exposure photographic plates.[[32]](#cite_note-dawn-community-36) This dramatically increased the rate of detection compared with earlier visual methods: Wolf alone discovered 248 asteroids, beginning with [323 Brucia](/wiki/323_Brucia "323 Brucia"),[[33]](#cite_note-37) whereas only slightly more than 300 had been discovered up to that point. It was known that there were many more, but most astronomers did not bother with them, some calling them "vermin of the skies",[[34]](#cite_note-38) a phrase variously attributed to [Eduard Suess](/wiki/Eduard_Suess "Eduard Suess")[[35]](#cite_note-39) and [Edmund Weiss](/wiki/Edmund_Weiss "Edmund Weiss").[[36]](#cite_note-40) Even a century later, only a few thousand asteroids were identified, numbered and named.

### 19th and 20th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 19th and 20th centuries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Known_NEAs.svg/250px-Known_NEAs.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Known_NEAs.svg)

Cumulative discoveries of just the near-Earth asteroids known by size, 1980–2024

In the past, asteroids were discovered by a four-step process. First, a region of the sky was [photographed](/wiki/Astrophotography "Astrophotography") by a wide-field [telescope](/wiki/Telescope "Telescope") or [astrograph](/wiki/Astrograph "Astrograph"). Pairs of photographs were taken, typically one hour apart. Multiple pairs could be taken over a series of days. Second, the two films or [plates](/wiki/Photographic_plate "Photographic plate") of the same region were viewed under a [stereoscope](/wiki/Stereoscope "Stereoscope"). A body in orbit around the Sun would move slightly between the pair of films. Under the stereoscope, the image of the body would seem to float slightly above the background of stars. Third, once a moving body was identified, its location would be measured precisely using a digitizing microscope. The location would be measured relative to known star locations.[[37]](#cite_note-41)

These first three steps do not constitute asteroid discovery: the observer has only found an apparition, which gets a [provisional designation](/wiki/Provisional_designation_in_astronomy "Provisional designation in astronomy"), made up of the year of discovery, a letter representing the half-month of discovery, and finally a letter and a number indicating the discovery's sequential number (example: 1998 FJ74). The last step is sending the locations and time of observations to the [Minor Planet Center](/wiki/Minor_Planet_Center "Minor Planet Center"), where computer programs determine whether an apparition ties together earlier apparitions into a single orbit. If so, the object receives a catalogue number and the observer of the first apparition with a calculated orbit is declared the discoverer, and granted the honor of naming the object subject to the approval of the [International Astronomical Union](/wiki/International_Astronomical_Union "International Astronomical Union").[[38]](#cite_note-42)

## Naming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Naming")]

Main article: [Minor planet § Naming](/wiki/Minor_planet#Naming "Minor planet")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Asteroid20130318-full.jpg/250px-Asteroid20130318-full.jpg)](/wiki/File:Asteroid20130318-full.jpg)

[2013 EC](/wiki/2013_EC "2013 EC"), shown here in radar images, has a provisional designation

By 1851, the [Royal Astronomical Society](/wiki/Royal_Astronomical_Society "Royal Astronomical Society") decided that asteroids were being discovered at such a rapid rate that a different system was needed to categorize or name asteroids. In 1852, when [de Gasparis](/wiki/Annibale_de_Gasparis "Annibale de Gasparis") discovered the twentieth asteroid, [Benjamin Valz](/wiki/Benjamin_Valz "Benjamin Valz") gave it a name and a number designating its rank among asteroid discoveries, [20 Massalia](/wiki/20_Massalia "20 Massalia"). Sometimes asteroids were discovered and not seen again. So, starting in 1892, new asteroids were listed by the year and a capital letter indicating the order in which the asteroid's orbit was calculated and registered within that specific year. For example, the first two asteroids discovered in 1892 were labeled 1892A and 1892B. However, there were not enough letters in the alphabet for all of the asteroids discovered in 1893, so 1893Z was followed by 1893AA. A number of variations of these methods were tried, including designations that included year plus a Greek letter in 1914. A simple chronological numbering system was established in 1925.[[32]](#cite_note-dawn-community-36)[[39]](#cite_note-43)

Currently all newly discovered asteroids receive a [provisional designation](/wiki/Provisional_designation_in_astronomy "Provisional designation in astronomy") (such as [2002 AT4](/wiki/2002_AT4 "2002 AT4")) consisting of the year of discovery and an alphanumeric code indicating the [half-month](/wiki/Half-month "Half-month") of discovery and the sequence within that half-month. Once an asteroid's orbit has been confirmed, it is given a number, and later may also be given a name (e.g. [433 Eros](/wiki/433_Eros "433 Eros")). The formal naming convention uses parentheses around the number—e.g. (433) Eros—but dropping the parentheses is quite common. Informally, it is also common to drop the number altogether, or to drop it after the first mention when a name is repeated in running text.[[40]](#cite_note-OpenUNamingAstrds-44) In addition, names can be proposed by the asteroid's discoverer, within guidelines established by the International Astronomical Union.[[41]](#cite_note-PlanSocNameGuides-45)

### Symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Symbols")]

Main article: [Astronomical symbols](/wiki/Astronomical_symbols "Astronomical symbols")

The first asteroids to be discovered were assigned iconic symbols like the ones traditionally used to designate the planets. By 1852 there were two dozen asteroid symbols, which often occurred in multiple variants.[[42]](#cite_note-Gould-1852-46)

In 1851, after the fifteenth asteroid, [Eunomia](/wiki/15_Eunomia "15 Eunomia"), had been discovered, [Johann Franz Encke](/wiki/Johann_Franz_Encke "Johann Franz Encke") made a major change in the upcoming 1854 edition of the *[Berliner Astronomisches Jahrbuch](/wiki/Berliner_Astronomisches_Jahrbuch "Berliner Astronomisches Jahrbuch")* (BAJ, *Berlin Astronomical Yearbook*). He introduced a disk (circle), a traditional symbol for a star, as the generic symbol for an asteroid. The circle was then numbered in order of discovery to indicate a specific asteroid. The numbered-circle convention was quickly adopted by astronomers, and the next asteroid to be discovered ([16 Psyche](/wiki/16_Psyche "16 Psyche"), in 1852) was the first to be designated in that way at the time of its discovery. However, Psyche was given an iconic symbol as well, as were a few other asteroids discovered over the next few years. [20 Massalia](/wiki/20_Massalia "20 Massalia") was the first asteroid that was not assigned an iconic symbol, and no iconic symbols were created after the 1855 discovery of [37 Fides](/wiki/37_Fides "37 Fides").[[e]](#cite_note-47)[[43]](#cite_note-Hilton-2011-a-48)

## Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Formation")]

Main article: [Origin of the asteroid belt](/wiki/Origin_of_the_asteroid_belt "Origin of the asteroid belt")

Many asteroids are the shattered remnants of [planetesimals](/wiki/Planetesimal "Planetesimal"), bodies within the young Sun's [solar nebula](/wiki/Solar_nebula "Solar nebula") that never grew large enough to become [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet").[[44]](#cite_note-CNEOS-FAQ-49) It is thought that planetesimals in the asteroid belt evolved much like the rest of objects in the solar nebula until Jupiter neared its current mass, at which point excitation from [orbital resonances](/wiki/Orbital_resonance "Orbital resonance") with Jupiter ejected over 99% of planetesimals in the belt. Simulations and a discontinuity in spin rate and spectral properties suggest that asteroids larger than approximately 120 km (75 mi) in diameter [accreted](/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics) "Accretion (astrophysics)") during that early era, whereas smaller bodies are fragments from collisions between asteroids during or after the Jovian disruption.[[45]](#cite_note-50) Ceres and Vesta grew large enough to melt and [differentiate](/wiki/Planetary_differentiation "Planetary differentiation"), with heavy metallic elements sinking to the core, leaving rocky minerals in the crust.[[46]](#cite_note-ACM-51)

In the [Nice model](/wiki/Nice_model "Nice model"), many [Kuiper-belt objects](/wiki/Kuiper_belt "Kuiper belt") are captured in the outer asteroid belt, at distances greater than 2.6 AU. Most were later ejected by Jupiter, but those that remained may be the [D-type asteroids](/wiki/D-type_asteroid "D-type asteroid"), and possibly include Ceres.[[47]](#cite_note-52)

## Distribution within the Solar System

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Distribution within the Solar System")]

See also: [List of minor-planet groups](/wiki/List_of_minor-planet_groups "List of minor-planet groups"), [List of notable asteroids](/wiki/List_of_notable_asteroids "List of notable asteroids"), and [List of minor planets](/wiki/List_of_minor_planets "List of minor planets")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Inner_solar_system_objects_top_view_for_wiki.png/500px-Inner_solar_system_objects_top_view_for_wiki.png)](/wiki/File:Inner_solar_system_objects_top_view_for_wiki.png)

A top view of asteroid group location in the inner solar system

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Inner_solar_system_linear_map.png/250px-Inner_solar_system_linear_map.png)](/wiki/File:Inner_solar_system_linear_map.png)

A map of planets and asteroid groups of the inner solar system. Distances from sun are to scale, object sizes are not.

Various dynamical groups of asteroids have been discovered orbiting in the inner Solar System. Their orbits are perturbed by the gravity of other bodies in the Solar System and by the [Yarkovsky effect](/wiki/Yarkovsky_effect "Yarkovsky effect"). Significant populations include:

### Asteroid belt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Asteroid belt")]

Main article: [Asteroid belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt")

The majority of known asteroids orbit within the asteroid belt between the orbits of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") and [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), generally in relatively low-[eccentricity](/wiki/Orbital_eccentricity "Orbital eccentricity") (i.e. not very elongated) orbits. This belt is estimated to contain between 1.1 and 1.9 million asteroids larger than 1 km (0.6 mi) in diameter,[[48]](#cite_note-53) and millions of smaller ones. These asteroids may be remnants of the [protoplanetary disk](/wiki/Protoplanetary_disk "Protoplanetary disk"), and in this region the [accretion](/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics) "Accretion (astrophysics)") of [planetesimals](/wiki/Planetesimal "Planetesimal") into planets during the formative period of the Solar System was prevented by large gravitational perturbations by [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter").

Contrary to popular imagery, the asteroid belt is mostly empty. The asteroids are spread over such a large volume that reaching an asteroid without aiming carefully would be improbable. Nonetheless, hundreds of thousands of asteroids are currently known, and the total number ranges in the millions or more, depending on the lower size cutoff. Over 200 asteroids are known to be larger than 100 km,[[49]](#cite_note-54) and a survey in the infrared wavelengths has shown that the asteroid belt has between 700,000 and 1.7 million asteroids with a diameter of 1 km or more.[[50]](#cite_note-55) The [absolute magnitudes](/wiki/Absolute_magnitude "Absolute magnitude") of most of the known asteroids are between 11 and 19, with the median at about 16.[[51]](#cite_note-mpc-56)

The total mass of the asteroid belt is estimated to be 2.39×1021 kg, which is just 3% of the mass of the Moon; the mass of the [Kuiper Belt](/wiki/Kuiper_belt "Kuiper belt") and [Scattered Disk](/wiki/Scattered_disc "Scattered disc") is over 100 times as large.[[52]](#cite_note-Pitjeva2018-57) The four largest objects, Ceres, Vesta, Pallas, and [Hygiea](/wiki/10_Hygiea "10 Hygiea"), account for maybe 62% of the belt's total mass, with 39% accounted for by Ceres alone.

### Trojans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Trojans")]

Main article: [Trojan (celestial body)](/wiki/Trojan_(celestial_body) "Trojan (celestial body)")

Trojans are populations that share an orbit with a larger planet or moon, but do not collide with it because they orbit in one of the two [Lagrangian points](/wiki/Lagrangian_point "Lagrangian point") of stability, L4 and L5, which lie 60° ahead of and behind the larger body.

In the Solar System, most known trojans share the [orbit of Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter_trojan "Jupiter trojan"). They are divided into the [Greek camp](/wiki/Greek_camp "Greek camp") at L4 (ahead of Jupiter) and the [Trojan camp](/wiki/Trojan_camp "Trojan camp") at L5 (trailing Jupiter). More than a million Jupiter trojans larger than one kilometer are thought to exist,[[53]](#cite_note-Yoshida2006-58) of which more than 7,000 are currently catalogued. In other planetary orbits only nine [Mars trojans](/wiki/Mars_trojan "Mars trojan"), 28 [Neptune trojans](/wiki/Neptune_trojan "Neptune trojan"), two [Uranus trojans](/wiki/Uranus_trojan "Uranus trojan"), and two [Earth trojans](/wiki/Earth_trojan "Earth trojan"), have been found to date. A temporary [Venus trojan](/wiki/2013_ND15 "2013 ND15") is also known. Numerical orbital dynamics stability simulations indicate that Saturn and Uranus probably do not have any primordial trojans.[[54]](#cite_note-sheppard2006-59)

### Near-Earth asteroids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Near-Earth asteroids")]

Main article: [Near-Earth asteroids](/wiki/Near-Earth_object#Near-Earth_asteroids "Near-Earth object")

[Near-Earth asteroids](/wiki/Near-Earth_asteroids "Near-Earth asteroids"), or NEAs, are asteroids that have orbits that pass close to that of Earth. Asteroids that actually cross Earth's orbital path are known as *Earth-crossers*. As of April 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit), a total of 28,772 near-Earth asteroids were known; 878 have a diameter of one kilometer or larger.[[55]](#cite_note-nasa_neo-60)

A small number of NEAs are [extinct comets](/wiki/Extinct_comets "Extinct comets") that have lost their volatile surface materials, although having a faint or intermittent comet-like tail does not necessarily result in a classification as a near-Earth comet, making the boundaries somewhat fuzzy. The rest of the near-Earth asteroids are driven out of the asteroid belt by gravitational interactions with [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter").[[56]](#cite_note-MorbidelliAstIII-61)[[57]](#cite_note-62)

Many asteroids have [natural satellites](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite") ([minor-planet moons](/wiki/Minor-planet_moon "Minor-planet moon")). As of October 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit), there were 85 NEAs known to have at least one moon, including three known to have two moons.[[58]](#cite_note-63) The asteroid [3122 Florence](/wiki/3122_Florence "3122 Florence"), one of the largest potentially hazardous asteroids with a diameter of 4.5 km (2.8 mi), has two moons measuring 100–300 m (330–980 ft) across, which were discovered by radar imaging during the asteroid's 2017 approach to Earth.[[59]](#cite_note-Florence-moons-64)

Near-Earth asteroids are divided into groups based on their [semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis") (a), [perihelion](/wiki/Apsis "Apsis") distance (q), and [aphelion](/wiki/Apsis "Apsis") distance (Q):[[60]](#cite_note-NEO-groups-65)[[56]](#cite_note-MorbidelliAstIII-61)

* The *[Atiras](/wiki/Atira_asteroid "Atira asteroid")* or *Apoheles* have orbits strictly inside Earth's orbit: an Atira asteroid's aphelion distance (Q) is smaller than Earth's perihelion distance (0.983 AU). That is, Q < 0.983 AU, which implies that the asteroid's semi-major axis is also less than 0.983 AU.[[61]](#cite_note-atiras-66)
* The *[Atens](/wiki/Aten_asteroid "Aten asteroid")* have a semi-major axis of less than 1 AU and cross Earth's orbit. Mathematically, a < 1.0 AU and Q > 0.983 AU. (0.983 AU is Earth's perihelion distance.)
* The *[Apollos](/wiki/Apollo_asteroid "Apollo asteroid")* have a semi-major axis of more than 1 AU and cross Earth's orbit. Mathematically, a > 1.0 AU and q < 1.017 AU. (1.017 AU is Earth's aphelion distance.)
* The *[Amors](/wiki/Amor_asteroid "Amor asteroid")* have orbits strictly outside Earth's orbit: an Amor asteroid's perihelion distance (q) is greater than Earth's aphelion distance (1.017 AU). Amor asteroids are also near-earth objects so q < 1.3 AU. In summary, 1.017 AU < q < 1.3 AU. (This implies that the asteroid's semi-major axis (a) is also larger than 1.017 AU.) Some Amor asteroid orbits cross the orbit of Mars.

### Martian moons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Martian moons")]

Main articles: [Moons of Mars](/wiki/Moons_of_Mars "Moons of Mars"), [Phobos (moon)](/wiki/Phobos_(moon) "Phobos (moon)"), and [Deimos (moon)](/wiki/Deimos_(moon) "Deimos (moon)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Phobos_colour_2008.jpg/250px-Phobos_colour_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Phobos_colour_2008.jpg)

Phobos

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Deimos-MRO.jpg/250px-Deimos-MRO.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deimos-MRO.jpg)

Deimos

It is unclear whether Martian moons [Phobos](/wiki/Phobos_(moon) "Phobos (moon)") and [Deimos](/wiki/Deimos_(moon) "Deimos (moon)") are captured asteroids or were formed due to impact event on Mars.[[62]](#cite_note-burns-67): 1284–1285 Phobos and Deimos both have much in common with carbonaceous [C-type asteroids](/wiki/C-type_asteroid "C-type asteroid"), with [spectra](/wiki/Electromagnetic_spectrum "Electromagnetic spectrum"), [albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo"), and [density](/wiki/Density "Density") very similar to those of C- or D-type asteroids.[[63]](#cite_note-c-type-68) Based on their similarity, one hypothesis is that both moons may be captured [main-belt asteroids](/wiki/Main-belt_asteroids "Main-belt asteroids").[[64]](#cite_note-69)[[65]](#cite_note-landis-70) Both moons have very circular orbits which lie almost exactly in Mars's [equatorial plane](/wiki/Equatorial_plane "Equatorial plane"), and hence a capture origin requires a mechanism for circularizing the initially highly eccentric orbit, and adjusting its inclination into the equatorial plane, most probably by a combination of atmospheric drag and [tidal forces](/wiki/Tidal_force "Tidal force"),[[66]](#cite_note-cazenave-71) although it is not clear whether sufficient time was available for this to occur for Deimos.[[62]](#cite_note-burns-67): 1300–1301 Capture also requires dissipation of energy. The current Martian atmosphere is too thin to capture a Phobos-sized object by atmospheric braking.[[62]](#cite_note-burns-67)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] [Geoffrey A. Landis](/wiki/Geoffrey_A._Landis "Geoffrey A. Landis") has pointed out that the capture could have occurred if the original body was a [binary asteroid](/wiki/Binary_asteroid "Binary asteroid") that separated under tidal forces.[[65]](#cite_note-landis-70)[[67]](#cite_note-72)

Phobos could be a second-generation Solar System object that [coalesced](/wiki/Accretion_(astrophysics) "Accretion (astrophysics)") in orbit after Mars formed, rather than forming concurrently out of the same birth cloud as Mars.[[68]](#cite_note-ESA2010-73)

Another hypothesis is that Mars was once surrounded by many Phobos- and Deimos-sized bodies, perhaps ejected into orbit around it by a collision with a large [planetesimal](/wiki/Planetesimal "Planetesimal").[[69]](#cite_note-Craddock-74) The high porosity of the interior of Phobos (based on the density of 1.88 g/cm3, voids are estimated to comprise 25 to 35 percent of Phobos's volume) is inconsistent with an asteroidal origin.[[70]](#cite_note-Andert-75) Observations of Phobos in the [thermal infrared](/wiki/Thermal_infrared "Thermal infrared") suggest a composition containing mainly [phyllosilicates](/wiki/Phyllosilicates "Phyllosilicates"), which are well known from the surface of Mars. The spectra are distinct from those of all classes of [chondrite](/wiki/Chondrite "Chondrite") meteorites, again pointing away from an asteroidal origin.[[71]](#cite_note-Giuranna-76) Both sets of findings support an origin of Phobos from material ejected by an impact on Mars that reaccreted in Martian orbit,[[72]](#cite_note-Blast-77) similar to the [prevailing theory](/wiki/Giant_impact_hypothesis "Giant impact hypothesis") for the origin of Earth's moon.

## Characteristics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Characteristics")]

### Size distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Size distribution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Asteroids_by_size_and_number.svg/330px-Asteroids_by_size_and_number.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Asteroids_by_size_and_number.svg)

The asteroids of the inner Solar System, categorized by size and number

CeresVestaPallasHygieaInteramniaEunomiaOtherCeresVestaPallasHygieaInteramniaEunomiaOtherLargest Asteroids in Main the Belt

The masses of the largest asteroids in the main belt: [1 Ceres](/wiki/1_Ceres "1 Ceres") (blue), [4 Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta"), [2 Pallas](/wiki/2_Pallas "2 Pallas"), [10 Hygiea](/wiki/10_Hygiea "10 Hygiea"), [704 Interamnia](/wiki/704_Interamnia "704 Interamnia"), [15 Eunomia](/wiki/15_Eunomia "15 Eunomia") and the remainder of the Main Belt (pink). The unit of mass is ×1018 kg.

Asteroids vary greatly in size, from almost 1,000 km (620 mi) for the largest down to rocks just 1 m (3.3 ft) across, below which an object is classified as a [meteoroid](/wiki/Meteoroid "Meteoroid").[[f]](#cite_note-78) The three largest are very much like miniature planets: they are roughly spherical, have at least partly differentiated interiors,[[73]](#cite_note-Schmidt2007-79) and are thought to be surviving [protoplanets](/wiki/Protoplanet "Protoplanet"). The vast majority, however, are much smaller and are irregularly shaped; they are thought to be either battered [planetesimals](/wiki/Planetesimal "Planetesimal") or fragments of larger bodies.

The [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet") [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)") is by far the largest asteroid, with a diameter of 940 km (580 mi). The next largest are [4 Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta") and [2 Pallas](/wiki/2_Pallas "2 Pallas"), both with diameters of just over 500 km (300 mi). Vesta is the brightest of the four main-belt asteroids that can, on occasion, be visible to the naked eye.[[74]](#cite_note-80) On some rare occasions, a near-Earth asteroid may briefly become visible without technical aid; see [99942 Apophis](/wiki/99942_Apophis "99942 Apophis").

The mass of all the objects of the [asteroid belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt"), lying between the orbits of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") and [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"), is estimated to be (2394±6)×1018 kg, ≈3.25% of the mass of the Moon. Of this, [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)") comprises 938×1018 kg, about 40% of the total. Adding in the next three most massive objects, [Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta") (11%), [Pallas](/wiki/2_Pallas "2 Pallas") (8.5%), and [Hygiea](/wiki/10_Hygiea "10 Hygiea") (3–4%), brings this figure up to a bit over 60%, whereas the next seven most-massive asteroids bring the total up to 70%.[[52]](#cite_note-Pitjeva2018-57) The number of asteroids increases rapidly as their individual masses decrease.

The number of asteroids decreases markedly with increasing size. Although the size distribution generally follows a [power law](/wiki/Power_law "Power law"), there are 'bumps' at about 5 km and 100 km, where more asteroids than expected from such a curve are found. Most asteroids larger than approximately 120 kilometres (75 mi) in diameter are primordial (surviving from the accretion epoch), whereas most smaller asteroids are products of fragmentation of primordial asteroids. The primordial population of the main belt was probably 200 times what it is today.[[75]](#cite_note-81)[[76]](#cite_note-82)

#### Largest asteroids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Largest asteroids")]

See also: [Largest asteroids](/wiki/Largest_asteroids "Largest asteroids")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/42_of_the_largest_objects_in_the_asteroid_belt.jpg/250px-42_of_the_largest_objects_in_the_asteroid_belt.jpg)](/wiki/File:42_of_the_largest_objects_in_the_asteroid_belt.jpg)

42 of the largest objects in the asteroid belt captured by [ESO](/wiki/European_Southern_Observatory "European Southern Observatory")'s [Very Large Telescope](/wiki/Very_Large_Telescope "Very Large Telescope")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Eros%2C_Vesta_and_Ceres_size_comparison.jpg/250px-Eros%2C_Vesta_and_Ceres_size_comparison.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eros,_Vesta_and_Ceres_size_comparison.jpg)

Eros, Vesta and Ceres size comparison

Three largest objects in the asteroid belt, [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)"), [Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta"), and [Pallas](/wiki/2_Pallas "2 Pallas"), are intact [protoplanets](/wiki/Protoplanet "Protoplanet") that share many characteristics common to planets, and are atypical compared to the majority of irregularly shaped asteroids. The fourth-largest asteroid, [Hygiea](/wiki/10_Hygiea "10 Hygiea"), appears nearly spherical although it may have an undifferentiated interior,[[77]](#cite_note-83) like the majority of asteroids. The four largest asteroids constitute about five-eighths of the mass of the asteroid belt; Ceres and Vesta alone constitute half.

Ceres is the only asteroid that appears to have a [plastic](/wiki/Plasticity_(physics) "Plasticity (physics)") shape under its own gravity and hence the only one that is a [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet").[[78]](#cite_note-IAU-2006-84) It has a much higher [absolute magnitude](/wiki/Absolute_magnitude#Solar_System_bodies_(H) "Absolute magnitude") than the other asteroids, of around 3.32,[[79]](#cite_note-AstJ-2002-v123-p549-85) and may possess a surface layer of ice.[[80]](#cite_note-planetary-86) Like the planets, Ceres is differentiated: it has a crust, a mantle and a core.[[80]](#cite_note-planetary-86) No meteorites from Ceres have been found on Earth.[[81]](#cite_note-satellites-87)

Vesta, too, has a differentiated interior, though it formed inside the Solar System's [frost line](/wiki/Frost_line_(astrophysics) "Frost line (astrophysics)"), and so is devoid of water;[[82]](#cite_note-88)[[83]](#cite_note-89) its composition is mainly of [basaltic](/wiki/Basaltic_rock "Basaltic rock") rock with minerals such as [olivine](/wiki/Olivine "Olivine").[[84]](#cite_note-90) Aside from the large crater at its southern pole, [Rheasilvia](/wiki/Rheasilvia "Rheasilvia"), Vesta also has an ellipsoidal shape. Vesta is the parent body of the [Vestian family](/wiki/Vestian_family "Vestian family") and other [V-type asteroids](/wiki/V-type_asteroid "V-type asteroid"), and is the source of the [HED meteorites](/wiki/HED_meteorite "HED meteorite"), which constitute 5% of all meteorites on Earth.

Pallas is unusual in that, like [Uranus](/wiki/Uranus "Uranus"), it rotates on its side, with its axis of rotation tilted at high angles to its orbital plane.[[85]](#cite_note-Torppa1996-91) Its composition is similar to that of Ceres: high in [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") and [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon"), and perhaps partially differentiated.[[86]](#cite_note-Icarus-1983-v56-p398-92) Pallas is the parent body of the [Palladian family](/wiki/Palladian_family "Palladian family") of asteroids.

Hygiea is the largest [carbonaceous](/wiki/Carbonaceous_asteroid "Carbonaceous asteroid") asteroid[[87]](#cite_note-Icarus-2002-156-p202-93) and, unlike the other largest asteroids, lies relatively close to the [plane of the ecliptic](/wiki/Plane_of_the_ecliptic "Plane of the ecliptic"). It is the largest member and presumed parent body of the [Hygiean family](/wiki/Hygiean_family "Hygiean family") of asteroids. Because there is no sufficiently large crater on the surface to be the source of that family, as there is on Vesta, it is thought that Hygiea may have been completely disrupted in the collision that formed the Hygiean family and recoalesced after losing a bit less than 2% of its mass. Observations taken with the [Very Large Telescope](/wiki/Very_Large_Telescope "Very Large Telescope")'s [SPHERE](/wiki/VLT-SPHERE "VLT-SPHERE") imager in 2017 and 2018, revealed that Hygiea has a nearly spherical shape, which is consistent both with it being in [hydrostatic equilibrium](/wiki/Hydrostatic_equilibrium "Hydrostatic equilibrium"), or formerly being in hydrostatic equilibrium, or with being disrupted and recoalescing.[[88]](#cite_note-NatAstr-2019-10-28-94)[[89]](#cite_note-Strickland2019-95)

Internal differentiation of large asteroids is possibly related to their lack of [natural satellites](/wiki/Natural_satellite "Natural satellite"), as satellites of main belt asteroids are mostly believed to form from collisional disruption, creating a [rubble pile](/wiki/Rubble_pile "Rubble pile") structure.[[81]](#cite_note-satellites-87)
