### Rotation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Rotation")]

Further information: [List of fast rotators (minor planets)](/wiki/List_of_fast_rotators_(minor_planets) "List of fast rotators (minor planets)") and [List of slow rotators (minor planets)](/wiki/List_of_slow_rotators_(minor_planets) "List of slow rotators (minor planets)")

Measurements of the rotation rates of large asteroids in the asteroid belt show that there is an upper limit. Very few asteroids with a diameter larger than 100 meters have a rotation period less than 2.2 hours.[[90]](#cite_note-96) For asteroids rotating faster than approximately this rate, the inertial force at the surface is greater than the gravitational force, so any loose surface material would be flung out. However, a solid object should be able to rotate much more rapidly. This suggests that most asteroids with a diameter over 100 meters are [rubble piles](/wiki/Rubble_pile "Rubble pile") formed through the accumulation of debris after collisions between asteroids.[[91]](#cite_note-Rossi-2004-97)

### Color

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Color")]

Asteroids become darker and redder with age due to [space weathering](/wiki/Space_weathering "Space weathering").[[92]](#cite_note-UHi2005-05-19-98) However evidence suggests most of the color change occurs rapidly, in the first hundred thousand years, limiting the usefulness of spectral measurement for determining the age of asteroids.[[93]](#cite_note-Courtland-2009-99)

### Surface features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Surface features")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Vesta_Cratered_terrain_with_hills_and_ridges.jpg/250px-Vesta_Cratered_terrain_with_hills_and_ridges.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vesta_Cratered_terrain_with_hills_and_ridges.jpg)

Cratered terrain on 4 Vesta

Except for the "[big four](/wiki/List_of_exceptional_asteroids#Largest_by_mass "List of exceptional asteroids")" (Ceres, Pallas, Vesta, and Hygiea), asteroids are likely to be broadly similar in appearance, if irregular in shape. 50 km (31 mi) [253 Mathilde](/wiki/253_Mathilde "253 Mathilde") is a rubble pile saturated with craters with diameters the size of the asteroid's radius. Earth-based observations of 300 km (190 mi) [511 Davida](/wiki/511_Davida "511 Davida"), one of the largest asteroids after the big four, reveal a similarly angular profile, suggesting it is also saturated with radius-size craters.[[94]](#cite_note-Icarus-2007-v191-p616-100) Medium-sized asteroids such as Mathilde and [243 Ida](/wiki/243_Ida "243 Ida"), that have been observed up close, also reveal a deep [regolith](/wiki/Regolith "Regolith") covering the surface. Of the big four, Pallas and Hygiea are practically unknown. Vesta has compression fractures encircling a radius-size crater at its south pole but is otherwise a [spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid").

*[Dawn spacecraft](/wiki/Dawn_(spacecraft) "Dawn (spacecraft)")* revealed that Ceres has a heavily cratered surface, but with fewer large craters than expected.[[95]](#cite_note-marchi-101) Models based on the formation of the current asteroid belt had suggested Ceres should possess 10 to 15 craters larger than 400 km (250 mi) in diameter.[[95]](#cite_note-marchi-101) The largest confirmed crater on Ceres, [Kerwan Basin](/wiki/Kerwan_(crater) "Kerwan (crater)"), is 284 km (176 mi) across.[[96]](#cite_note-102) The most likely reason for this is [viscous relaxation](/wiki/Viscoelasticity "Viscoelasticity") of the crust slowly flattening out larger impacts.[[95]](#cite_note-marchi-101)

### Composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Composition")]

Asteroids are classified by their characteristic [emission spectra](/wiki/Emission_spectrum "Emission spectrum"), with the majority falling into three main groups: [C-type](/wiki/C-type_asteroid "C-type asteroid"), [M-type](/wiki/M-type_asteroid "M-type asteroid"), and [S-type](/wiki/S-type_asteroid "S-type asteroid"). These describe carbonaceous ([carbon-rich](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon")), [metallic](/wiki/Metal "Metal"), and [silicaceous](/wiki/Silica "Silica") (stony) compositions, respectively. The physical composition of asteroids is varied and in most cases poorly understood. Ceres appears to be composed of a rocky core covered by an icy mantle; Vesta is thought to have a [nickel-iron](/wiki/Nickel-iron "Nickel-iron") core, [olivine](/wiki/Olivine "Olivine") mantle, and basaltic crust.[[97]](#cite_note-Hubble-Vespa-1995-04-19-103) Thought to be the largest undifferentiated asteroid, [10 Hygiea](/wiki/10_Hygiea "10 Hygiea") seems to have a uniformly primitive composition of [carbonaceous chondrite](/wiki/Carbonaceous_chondrite "Carbonaceous chondrite"), but it may actually be a differentiated asteroid that was globally disrupted by an impact and then reassembled. Other asteroids appear to be the remnant cores or mantles of proto-planets, high in rock and metal. Most small asteroids are believed to be piles of rubble held together loosely by gravity, although the largest are probably solid. Some asteroids have [moons](/wiki/Asteroid_moon "Asteroid moon") or are co-orbiting [binaries](/wiki/Binary_asteroid "Binary asteroid"): rubble piles, moons, binaries, and scattered [asteroid families](/wiki/Asteroid_family "Asteroid family") are thought to be the results of collisions that disrupted a parent asteroid, or possibly a [planet](/wiki/Disrupted_planet "Disrupted planet").[[98]](#cite_note-ARX-20060816-104)

In the main asteroid belt, there appear to be two primary populations of asteroid: a dark, volatile-rich population, consisting of the [C-type](/wiki/C-type_asteroid "C-type asteroid") and [P-type](/wiki/P-type_asteroid "P-type asteroid") asteroids, with albedos less than 0.10 and densities under 2.2 g/cm3, and a dense, volatile-poor population, consisting of the [S-type](/wiki/S-type_asteroid "S-type asteroid") and [M-type](/wiki/M-type_asteroid "M-type asteroid") asteroids, with albedos over 0.15 and densities greater than 2.7. Within these populations, larger asteroids are denser, presumably due to compression. There appears to be minimal macro-porosity (interstitial vacuum) in the score of asteroids with masses greater than 10×1018 kg.[[99]](#cite_note-VLT-105)

Composition is calculated from three primary sources: [albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo"), surface spectrum, and density. The last can only be determined accurately by observing the orbits of moons the asteroid might have. So far, every asteroid with moons has turned out to be a rubble pile, a loose conglomeration of rock and metal that may be half empty space by volume. The investigated asteroids are as large as 280 km in diameter, and include [121 Hermione](/wiki/121_Hermione "121 Hermione") (268×186×183 km), and [87 Sylvia](/wiki/87_Sylvia "87 Sylvia") (384×262×232 km). Few asteroids are [larger than 87 Sylvia](/wiki/List_of_notable_asteroids#Largest_by_diameter "List of notable asteroids"), none of them have moons. The fact that such large asteroids as Sylvia may be rubble piles, presumably due to disruptive impacts, has important consequences for the formation of the Solar System: computer simulations of collisions involving solid bodies show them destroying each other as often as merging, but colliding rubble piles are more likely to merge. This means that the cores of the planets could have formed relatively quickly.[[100]](#cite_note-Icarus-2011-02-p1022-106)

#### Water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Water")]

Main article: [Asteroidal water](/wiki/Asteroidal_water "Asteroidal water")

Scientists hypothesize that some of the first water brought to Earth was delivered by asteroid impacts after the collision that produced the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon").[[101]](#cite_note-Campins2010-107) In 2009, the presence of [water ice](/wiki/Ice "Ice") was confirmed on the surface of [24 Themis](/wiki/24_Themis "24 Themis") using NASA's [Infrared Telescope Facility](/wiki/Infrared_Telescope_Facility "Infrared Telescope Facility"). The surface of the asteroid appears completely covered in ice. As this ice layer is [sublimating](/wiki/Sublimation_(phase_transition) "Sublimation (phase transition)"), it may be getting replenished by a reservoir of ice under the surface. Organic compounds were also detected on the surface.[[102]](#cite_note-Cowen-2009-108)[[103]](#cite_note-Atkinson-2009-109)[[101]](#cite_note-Campins2010-107)[[104]](#cite_note-RivkinEmery2010-110) The presence of ice on 24 Themis makes the initial theory plausible.[[101]](#cite_note-Campins2010-107)

In October 2013, water was detected on an extrasolar body for the first time, on an asteroid orbiting the [white dwarf](/wiki/White_dwarf "White dwarf") [GD 61](/wiki/GD_61 "GD 61").[[105]](#cite_note-111) On 22 January 2014, [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency") (ESA) scientists reported the detection, for the first definitive time, of [water vapor](/wiki/Water_vapor "Water vapor") on [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)"), the largest object in the asteroid belt.[[106]](#cite_note-KüppersO'Rourke2014-112) The detection was made by using the [far-infrared abilities](/wiki/Far-infrared_astronomy "Far-infrared astronomy") of the [Herschel Space Observatory](/wiki/Herschel_Space_Observatory "Herschel Space Observatory").[[107]](#cite_note-NASA-20140122-113) The finding is unexpected because comets, not asteroids, are typically considered to "sprout jets and plumes". According to one of the scientists, "The lines are becoming more and more blurred between comets and asteroids."[[107]](#cite_note-NASA-20140122-113)

Findings have shown that [solar winds](/wiki/Solar_wind "Solar wind") can react with the oxygen in the upper layer of the asteroids and create water. It has been estimated that "every cubic metre of irradiated rock could contain up to 20 litres"; study was conducted using an atom probe tomography, numbers are given for the Itokawa S-type asteroid.[[108]](#cite_note-114)[[109]](#cite_note-115)

Acfer 049, a meteorite discovered in Algeria in 1990, was shown in 2019 to have an ultraporous lithology (UPL): porous texture that could be formed by removal of ice that filled these pores, this suggests that UPL "represent fossils of primordial ice".[[110]](#cite_note-116)

#### Organic compounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Organic compounds")]

Asteroids contain traces of [amino acids](/wiki/Amino_acid "Amino acid") and other organic compounds, and some speculate that asteroid impacts may have seeded the early Earth with the chemicals necessary to initiate life, or may have even brought life itself to Earth (an event called "[panspermia](/wiki/Panspermia "Panspermia")").[[111]](#cite_note-SPACE-2001-12-19-117)[[112]](#cite_note-Reuell-2019-118) In August 2011, a report, based on [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") studies with [meteorites](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite") found on [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"), was published suggesting [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") and [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA") components ([adenine](/wiki/Adenine "Adenine"), [guanine](/wiki/Guanine "Guanine") and related [organic molecules](/wiki/Organic_molecules "Organic molecules")) may have been formed on asteroids and [comets](/wiki/Comet "Comet") in [outer space](/wiki/Outer_space "Outer space").[[113]](#cite_note-Callahan-119)[[114]](#cite_note-Steigerwald-120)[[115]](#cite_note-DNA-121)

In November 2019, scientists reported detecting, for the first time, [sugar molecules](/wiki/Sugar "Sugar"), including [ribose](/wiki/Ribose "Ribose"), in [meteorites](/wiki/Meteorite "Meteorite"), suggesting that chemical processes on asteroids can produce some fundamentally essential bio-ingredients important to [life](/wiki/Life "Life"), and supporting the notion of an [RNA world](/wiki/RNA_world "RNA world") prior to a DNA-based [origin of life](/wiki/Abiogenesis "Abiogenesis") on Earth, and possibly, as well, the notion of [panspermia](/wiki/Panspermia "Panspermia").[[116]](#cite_note-NASA-20191118-122)[[117]](#cite_note-PNAS-20191118-123)[[118]](#cite_note-124)

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Classification")]

Asteroids are commonly categorized according to two criteria: the characteristics of their orbits, and features of their reflectance [spectrum](/wiki/Visible_spectrum "Visible spectrum").

### Orbital classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Orbital classification")]

Main articles: [Asteroid group](/wiki/Asteroid_group "Asteroid group") and [Asteroid family](/wiki/Asteroid_family "Asteroid family")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Animation_of_%28419624%29_2010_SO16_orbit.gif/250px-Animation_of_%28419624%29_2010_SO16_orbit.gif)](/wiki/File:Animation_of_(419624)_2010_SO16_orbit.gif)

A complex horseshoe orbit (the vertical looping is due to inclination of the smaller body's orbit to that of the Earth, and would be absent if both orbited in the same plane)  Sun **·**   Earth **·**   [(419624) 2010 SO16](/wiki/(419624)_2010_SO16 "(419624) 2010 SO16")

Many asteroids have been placed in groups and families based on their orbital characteristics. Apart from the broadest divisions, it is customary to name a group of asteroids after the first member of that group to be discovered. Groups are relatively loose dynamical associations, whereas families are tighter and result from the catastrophic break-up of a large parent asteroid sometime in the past.[[119]](#cite_note-AstFams-Icarus-1995-125) Families are more common and easier to identify within the main asteroid belt, but several small families have been reported among the [Jupiter trojans](/wiki/Jupiter_trojan "Jupiter trojan").[[120]](#cite_note-JewittEtal2004-126) Main belt families were first recognized by [Kiyotsugu Hirayama](/wiki/Kiyotsugu_Hirayama "Kiyotsugu Hirayama") in 1918 and are often called [Hirayama families](/wiki/Hirayama_families "Hirayama families") in his honor.

About 30–35% of the bodies in the asteroid belt belong to dynamical families, each thought to have a common origin in a past collision between asteroids. A family has also been associated with the plutoid [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet") [Haumea](/wiki/Haumea "Haumea").

Some asteroids have unusual [horseshoe orbits](/wiki/Horseshoe_orbit "Horseshoe orbit") that are co-orbital with [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") or another planet. Examples are [3753 Cruithne](/wiki/3753_Cruithne "3753 Cruithne") and [2002 AA29](/wiki/2002_AA29 "2002 AA29"). The first instance of this type of orbital arrangement was discovered between [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn "Saturn")'s moons [Epimetheus](/wiki/Epimetheus_(moon) "Epimetheus (moon)") and [Janus](/wiki/Janus_(moon) "Janus (moon)"). Sometimes these horseshoe objects temporarily become [quasi-satellites](/wiki/Quasi-satellite "Quasi-satellite") for a few decades or a few hundred years, before returning to their earlier status. Both Earth and [Venus](/wiki/Venus "Venus") are known to have quasi-satellites.

Such objects, if associated with Earth or Venus or even hypothetically [Mercury](/wiki/Mercury_(planet) "Mercury (planet)"), are a special class of [Aten asteroids](/wiki/Aten_asteroid "Aten asteroid"). However, such objects could be associated with the outer planets as well.

### Spectral classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Spectral classification")]

Main article: [Asteroid spectral types](/wiki/Asteroid_spectral_types "Asteroid spectral types")

In 1975, an asteroid [taxonomic](/wiki/Taxonomy_(general) "Taxonomy (general)") system based on [color](/wiki/Color "Color"), [albedo](/wiki/Albedo "Albedo"), and [spectral shape](/wiki/Spectral_line "Spectral line") was developed by [Chapman](/wiki/Clark_R._Chapman "Clark R. Chapman"), [Morrison](/wiki/David_Morrison_(astrophysicist) "David Morrison (astrophysicist)"), and [Zellner](/w/index.php?title=Ben_Zellner&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ben Zellner (page does not exist)").[[121]](#cite_note-CMZ-1975-Icarus-127) These properties are thought to correspond to the composition of the asteroid's surface material. The original classification system had three categories: [C-types](/wiki/C-type_asteroid "C-type asteroid") for dark carbonaceous objects (75% of known asteroids), [S-types](/wiki/S-type_asteroid "S-type asteroid") for stony (silicaceous) objects (17% of known asteroids) and U for those that did not fit into either C or S. This classification has since been expanded to include many other asteroid types. The number of types continues to grow as more asteroids are studied.

The two most widely used taxonomies now used are the [Tholen classification](/wiki/Tholen_classification "Tholen classification") and [SMASS classification](/wiki/SMASS_classification "SMASS classification"). The former was proposed in 1984 by [David J. Tholen](/wiki/David_J._Tholen "David J. Tholen"), and was based on data collected from an eight-color asteroid survey performed in the 1980s. This resulted in 14 asteroid categories.[[122]](#cite_note-Tholen-1989-128) In 2002, the Small Main-Belt Asteroid Spectroscopic Survey resulted in a modified version of the Tholen taxonomy with 24 different types. Both systems have three broad categories of C, S, and X asteroids, where X consists of mostly metallic asteroids, such as the [M-type](/wiki/M-type_asteroid "M-type asteroid"). There are also several smaller classes.[[123]](#cite_note-Bus-2002-129)

The proportion of known asteroids falling into the various spectral types does not necessarily reflect the proportion of all asteroids that are of that type; some types are easier to detect than others, biasing the totals.

#### Problems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Problems")]

Originally, spectral designations were based on inferences of an asteroid's composition.[[124]](#cite_note-McSween-1999-130) However, the correspondence between spectral class and composition is not always very good, and a variety of classifications are in use. This has led to significant confusion. Although asteroids of different spectral classifications are likely to be composed of different materials, there are no assurances that asteroids within the same taxonomic class are composed of the same (or similar) materials.

### Active asteroids

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Active asteroids")]

Main article: [Active asteroid](/wiki/Active_asteroid "Active asteroid")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/PIA23554-AsteroidBennu-EjectingParticles-20190106.jpg/250px-PIA23554-AsteroidBennu-EjectingParticles-20190106.jpg)](/wiki/File:PIA23554-AsteroidBennu-EjectingParticles-20190106.jpg)

Asteroid [(101955) Bennu](/wiki/101955_Bennu "101955 Bennu") seen ejecting particles by the [OSIRIS-REx](/wiki/OSIRIS-REx "OSIRIS-REx")

Active asteroids are objects that have asteroid-like orbits but show [comet](/wiki/Comet "Comet")-like visual characteristics. That is, they show [comae](/wiki/Coma_(cometary) "Coma (cometary)"), [tails](/wiki/Comet_tail "Comet tail"), or other visual evidence of mass-loss (like a comet), but their orbit remains within [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter")'s orbit (like an asteroid).[[125]](#cite_note-Jewitt-131)[[126]](#cite_note-JHA15-132) These bodies were originally designated **main-belt comets** (MBCs) in 2006 by astronomers [David C. Jewitt](/wiki/David_C._Jewitt "David C. Jewitt") and [Henry Hsieh](/w/index.php?title=Henry_Hsieh_(astronomer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Henry Hsieh (astronomer) (page does not exist)"), but this name implies they are necessarily icy in composition like a comet and that they only exist within the [main-belt](/wiki/Asteroid_belt "Asteroid belt"), whereas the growing population of active asteroids shows that this is not always the case.[[125]](#cite_note-Jewitt-131)[[127]](#cite_note-NYT-20190319-133)[[128]](#cite_note-134)

The first active asteroid discovered is [7968 Elst–Pizarro](/wiki/7968_Elst%E2%80%93Pizarro "7968 Elst–Pizarro"). It was discovered (as an asteroid) in 1979 but then was found to have a tail by [Eric Elst](/wiki/Eric_Walter_Elst "Eric Walter Elst") and Guido Pizarro in 1996 and given the cometary designation 133P/Elst-Pizarro.[[125]](#cite_note-Jewitt-131)[[129]](#cite_note-HH133P-135) Another notable object is [311P/PanSTARRS](/wiki/311P/PanSTARRS "311P/PanSTARRS"): observations made by the [Hubble Space Telescope](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope") revealed that it had six comet-like tails.[[130]](#cite_note-hubblesite-136) The tails are suspected to be streams of material ejected by the asteroid as a result of a [rubble pile](/wiki/Rubble_pile "Rubble pile") asteroid spinning fast enough to remove material from it.[[131]](#cite_note-Jewitt2013-137)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Hubble_view_of_Dimorphos_ejecta_%28October_8%29.png/250px-Hubble_view_of_Dimorphos_ejecta_%28October_8%29.png)](/wiki/File:Hubble_view_of_Dimorphos_ejecta_(October_8).png)

Dimorphos and the tail created after the DART impact, photo by the Hubble Space Telescope

By smashing into the asteroid [Dimorphos](/wiki/Dimorphos "Dimorphos"), NASA's [Double Asteroid Redirection Test](/wiki/Double_Asteroid_Redirection_Test "Double Asteroid Redirection Test") spacecraft made it an active asteroid. Scientists had proposed that some active asteroids are the result of impact events, but no one had ever observed the activation of an asteroid. The DART mission activated Dimorphos under precisely known and carefully observed impact conditions, enabling the detailed study of the formation of an active asteroid for the first time.[[132]](#cite_note-nasa-march2023-138)[[133]](#cite_note-139) Observations show that Dimorphos lost approximately 1 million kilograms after the collision.[[134]](#cite_note-140) Impact produced a dust plume that temporarily brightened the Didymos system and developed a 10,000-kilometer (6,200 mi)-long [dust tail](/wiki/Comet_tail "Comet tail") that persisted for several months.[[135]](#cite_note-NOIRLab-20221003-141)[[136]](#cite_note-NASA-20221215-142)[[137]](#cite_note-Li2023-143)

### Dark comets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Dark comets")]

Main article: [Dark comet](/wiki/Dark_comet "Dark comet")

First identified in 2024, dark comets are asteroids that show non-gravitational acceleration, a behavior normally seen in comets but not asteroids, but have no coma or tail, appearing visually as asteroids. Two families have been identified. The Outer Family consists of objects larger than 100 meters, and have orbits that reach out nearly to Jupiter, resembling [Jupiter family comets](/wiki/Jupiter_family_comets "Jupiter family comets"). The Inner Family dark comets were smaller than 50 meters, darker, and had roughly circular orbits.[[138]](#cite_note-dark_comets-144) All dark comets orbit in the main asteroid belt except for [ʻOumuamua](/wiki/%CA%BBOumuamua "ʻOumuamua"), an interstellar object, which was the first asteroid-like object to show non-gravitation acceleration, while appearing visually as an asteroid.[[139]](#cite_note-non-grav-145)

## Observation and exploration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Observation and exploration")]

Until the age of space travel, objects in the asteroid belt could only be observed with large telescopes, their shapes and terrain remaining a mystery. The best modern ground-based telescopes and the Earth-orbiting [Hubble Space Telescope](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope") can only resolve a small amount of detail on the surfaces of the largest asteroids. Limited information about the shapes and compositions of asteroids can be inferred from their [light curves](/wiki/Light_curve "Light curve") (variation in brightness during rotation) and their spectral properties. Sizes can be estimated by timing the lengths of star occultations (when an asteroid passes directly in front of a star). [Radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") imaging can yield good information about asteroid shapes and orbital and rotational parameters, especially for near-Earth asteroids. Spacecraft flybys can provide much more data than any ground or space-based observations; sample-return missions gives insights about regolith composition.

### Ground-based observations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Ground-based observations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Goldstone_DSN_antenna.jpg/250px-Goldstone_DSN_antenna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goldstone_DSN_antenna.jpg)

The 70m antenna at Goldstone Observatory

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/2014_SR339_Arecibo.jpg/250px-2014_SR339_Arecibo.jpg)](/wiki/File:2014_SR339_Arecibo.jpg)

Radar observations of near-Earth asteroid [(505657) 2014 SR339](/wiki/(505657)_2014_SR339 "(505657) 2014 SR339") as seen by Arecibo

As asteroids are rather small and faint objects, the data that can be obtained from ground-based observations (GBO) are limited. By means of ground-based optical telescopes the visual magnitude can be obtained; when converted into the absolute magnitude it gives a rough estimate of the asteroid's size. Light-curve measurements can also be made by GBO; when collected over a long period of time it allows an estimate of the rotational period, the pole orientation (sometimes), and a rough estimate of the asteroid's shape. Spectral data (both visible-light and near-infrared spectroscopy) gives information about the object's composition, used to classify the observed asteroids. Such observations are limited as they provide information about only the thin layer on the surface (up to several micrometers).[[140]](#cite_note-michel-146) As planetologist [Patrick Michel](/wiki/Patrick_Michel "Patrick Michel") writes:

> Mid- to thermal-infrared observations, along with polarimetry measurements, are probably the only data that give some indication of actual physical properties. Measuring the heat flux of an asteroid at a single wavelength gives an estimate of the dimensions of the object; these measurements have lower uncertainty than measurements of the reflected sunlight in the visible-light spectral region. If the two measurements can be combined, both the effective diameter and the geometric albedo—the latter being a measure of the brightness at zero phase angle, that is, when illumination comes from directly behind the observer—can be derived. In addition, thermal measurements at two or more wavelengths, plus the brightness in the visible-light region, give information on the thermal properties. The thermal inertia, which is a measure of how fast a material heats up or cools off, of most observed asteroids is lower than the bare-rock reference value but greater than that of the lunar regolith; this observation indicates the presence of an insulating layer of granular material on their surface.[[140]](#cite_note-michel-146)

Near-Earth asteroids that come into close vicinity of the planet can be studied in more details with [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar"); it provides information about the surface of the asteroid (for example can show the presence of craters and boulders). Such observations were conducted by the [Arecibo Observatory](/wiki/Arecibo_Observatory "Arecibo Observatory") in Puerto Rico (305 meter dish) and [Goldstone Observatory](/wiki/Goldstone_Observatory "Goldstone Observatory") in California (70 meter dish). Radar observations can also be used for accurate determination of the orbital and rotational dynamics of observed objects.[[140]](#cite_note-michel-146)

### Space-based observations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Space-based observations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/WISE_artist_concept_%28PIA17254%2C_crop%29.jpg/250px-WISE_artist_concept_%28PIA17254%2C_crop%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:WISE_artist_concept_(PIA17254,_crop).jpg)

[WISE](/wiki/Wide-field_Infrared_Survey_Explorer "Wide-field Infrared Survey Explorer") infrared space telescope

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Asteroid_6481_Tenzing_captured_by_JWST.gif/250px-Asteroid_6481_Tenzing_captured_by_JWST.gif)](/wiki/File:Asteroid_6481_Tenzing_captured_by_JWST.gif)

Asteroid [6481 Tenzing](/wiki/6481_Tenzing "6481 Tenzing"), center, is seen moving against a background of stars in this series of images taken by the [James Webb Space Telescope](/wiki/James_Webb_Space_Telescope "James Webb Space Telescope")'s instrument [NIRCam](/wiki/NIRCam "NIRCam").

Both space and ground-based observatories conducted asteroid search programs; the space-based searches are expected to detect more objects because there is no atmosphere to interfere and because they can observe larger portions of the sky. [NEOWISE](/wiki/NEOWISE "NEOWISE") observed more than 100,000 asteroids of the main belt, [Spitzer Space Telescope](/wiki/Spitzer_Space_Telescope "Spitzer Space Telescope") observed more than 700 near-Earth asteroids. These observations determined rough sizes of the majority of observed objects, but provided limited detail about surface properties (such as regolith depth and composition, angle of repose, cohesion, and porosity).[[140]](#cite_note-michel-146)

Asteroids were also studied by the [Hubble Space Telescope](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope"), such as tracking the colliding asteroids in the main belt,[[141]](#cite_note-147)[[142]](#cite_note-148) break-up of an asteroid,[[143]](#cite_note-149) observing an [active asteroid](/wiki/Active_asteroid "Active asteroid") with six comet-like tails,[[144]](#cite_note-150) and observing asteroids that were chosen as targets of dedicated missions.[[145]](#cite_note-151)[[146]](#cite_note-152)

### Space probe missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Space probe missions")]

See also: [List of minor planets and comets visited by spacecraft](/wiki/List_of_minor_planets_and_comets_visited_by_spacecraft "List of minor planets and comets visited by spacecraft") and [List of missions to minor planets](/wiki/List_of_missions_to_minor_planets "List of missions to minor planets")

According to [Patrick Michel](/wiki/Patrick_Michel "Patrick Michel")

> The internal structure of asteroids is inferred only from indirect evidence: bulk densities measured by spacecraft, the orbits of natural satellites in the case of asteroid binaries, and the drift of an asteroid's orbit due to the Yarkovsky thermal effect. A spacecraft near an asteroid is perturbed enough by the asteroid's gravity to allow an estimate of the asteroid's mass. The volume is then estimated using a model of the asteroid's shape. Mass and volume allow the derivation of the bulk density, whose uncertainty is usually dominated by the errors made on the volume estimate. The internal porosity of asteroids can be inferred by comparing their bulk density with that of their assumed meteorite analogues, dark asteroids seem to be more porous (>40%) than bright ones. The nature of this porosity is unclear.[[140]](#cite_note-michel-146)

#### Dedicated missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Dedicated missions")]

The first asteroid to be photographed in close-up was [951 Gaspra](/wiki/951_Gaspra "951 Gaspra") in 1991, followed in 1993 by [243 Ida](/wiki/243_Ida "243 Ida") and its moon [Dactyl](/wiki/Dactyl_(asteroid) "Dactyl (asteroid)"), all of which were imaged by the [*Galileo* probe](/wiki/Galileo_(spacecraft) "Galileo (spacecraft)") en route to [Jupiter](/wiki/Jupiter "Jupiter"). Other asteroids briefly visited by spacecraft en route to other destinations include [9969 Braille](/wiki/9969_Braille "9969 Braille") (by *[Deep Space 1](/wiki/Deep_Space_1 "Deep Space 1")* in 1999), [5535 Annefrank](/wiki/5535_Annefrank "5535 Annefrank") (by *[Stardust](/wiki/Stardust_(spacecraft) "Stardust (spacecraft)")* in 2002), [2867 Šteins](/wiki/2867_%C5%A0teins "2867 Šteins") and [21 Lutetia](/wiki/21_Lutetia "21 Lutetia") (by the [*Rosetta* probe](/wiki/Rosetta_(spacecraft) "Rosetta (spacecraft)") in 2008), and [4179 Toutatis](/wiki/4179_Toutatis "4179 Toutatis") (China's lunar orbiter *[Chang'e 2](/wiki/Chang%27e_2 "Chang'e 2")*, which flew within 3.2 km (2 mi) in 2012).

The first dedicated asteroid probe was NASA's *[NEAR Shoemaker](/wiki/NEAR_Shoemaker "NEAR Shoemaker")*, which photographed [253 Mathilde](/wiki/253_Mathilde "253 Mathilde") in 1997, before entering into orbit around [433 Eros](/wiki/433_Eros "433 Eros"), finally landing on its surface in 2001. It was the first spacecraft to successfully orbit and land on an asteroid.[[147]](#cite_note-twofirsts-153) From September to November 2005, the Japanese *[Hayabusa](/wiki/Hayabusa_(spacecraft) "Hayabusa (spacecraft)")* probe studied [25143 Itokawa](/wiki/25143_Itokawa "25143 Itokawa") in detail and [returned samples](/wiki/Sample_return_mission "Sample return mission") of its surface to Earth on 13 June 2010, the first asteroid sample-return mission. In 2007, [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") launched the [*Dawn*](/wiki/Dawn_(spacecraft) "Dawn (spacecraft)") spacecraft, which orbited [4 Vesta](/wiki/4_Vesta "4 Vesta") for a year, and observed the dwarf planet [Ceres](/wiki/Ceres_(dwarf_planet) "Ceres (dwarf planet)") for three years.

*[Hayabusa2](/wiki/Hayabusa2 "Hayabusa2")*, a probe launched by [JAXA](/wiki/JAXA "JAXA") 2014, orbited its target asteroid [162173 Ryugu](/wiki/162173_Ryugu "162173 Ryugu") for more than a year and took samples that were delivered to Earth in 2020. The spacecraft is now on an extended mission and expected to arrive at a new target in 2031.

NASA launched the [OSIRIS-REx](/wiki/OSIRIS-REx "OSIRIS-REx") in 2016, a sample return mission to asteroid [101955 Bennu](/wiki/101955_Bennu "101955 Bennu"). In 2021, the probe departed the asteroid with a sample from its surface. Sample was delivered to Earth in September 2023. The spacecraft continues its extended mission, designated OSIRIS-APEX, to explore near-Earth asteroid Apophis in 2029.

In 2021, NASA launched [Double Asteroid Redirection Test](/wiki/Double_Asteroid_Redirection_Test "Double Asteroid Redirection Test") (DART), a mission to test technology for defending Earth against potential hazardous objects. DART deliberately crashed into the [minor-planet moon](/wiki/Minor-planet_moon "Minor-planet moon") [Dimorphos](/wiki/Dimorphos "Dimorphos") of the double asteroid [Didymos](/wiki/65803_Didymos "65803 Didymos") in September 2022 to assess the potential of a spacecraft impact to deflect an asteroid from a collision course with Earth.[[148]](#cite_note-154) In October, NASA declared DART a success, confirming it had shortened Dimorphos's orbital period around Didymos by about 32 minutes.[[149]](#cite_note-155)

NASA's *[Lucy](/wiki/Lucy_(spacecraft) "Lucy (spacecraft)")*, launched in 2021, is a multiple-asteroid flyby probe focused on flying by 7 [Jupiter trojans](/wiki/Jupiter_trojan "Jupiter trojan") of varying types. While not yet set to reach its first main target, [3548 Eurybates](/wiki/3548_Eurybates "3548 Eurybates"), until 2027, it has made flybys of the main-belt asteroids [152830 Dinkinesh](/wiki/152830_Dinkinesh "152830 Dinkinesh") and [52246 Donaldjohanson](/wiki/52246_Donaldjohanson "52246 Donaldjohanson").[[150]](#cite_note-156)[[151]](#cite_note-157)

NASA's *[Psyche](/wiki/Psyche_(spacecraft) "Psyche (spacecraft)")*, launched in October 2023, is intended to study the large metallic asteroid [of the same name](/wiki/16_Psyche "16 Psyche"), and is on track to arrive there in 2029.

ESA's *[Hera](/wiki/Hera_(space_mission) "Hera (space mission)")*, launched in October 2024, is intended study the results of the DART impact. It is expected to measure the size and morphology of the crater, and momentum transmitted by the impact, to determine the efficiency of the deflection produced by DART.

CNSA's *[Tianwen-2](/wiki/Tianwen-2 "Tianwen-2")* was launched in May 2025.[[6]](#cite_note-auto-6) It will use [solar electric propulsion](/wiki/Solar_electric_propulsion "Solar electric propulsion") to explore the [co-orbital](/wiki/Co-orbital "Co-orbital") near-Earth asteroid [469219 Kamoʻoalewa](/wiki/469219_Kamo%CA%BBoalewa "469219 Kamoʻoalewa") and the [active asteroid](/wiki/Active_asteroid "Active asteroid") [311P/PanSTARRS](/wiki/311P/PanSTARRS "311P/PanSTARRS"). The spacecraft is tasked with collecting samples of the regolith of Kamo'oalewa.[[7]](#cite_note-nature20190430-7)

#### Planned missions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Planned missions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Small_Asteroids_and_Comets_Visited_by_Spacecraft.jpg/330px-Small_Asteroids_and_Comets_Visited_by_Spacecraft.jpg)](/wiki/File:Small_Asteroids_and_Comets_Visited_by_Spacecraft.jpg)

Asteroids and comets visited by spacecraft as of 2019 (except Ceres and Vesta), to scale

* JAXA's [DESTINY+](/wiki/DESTINY%2B "DESTINY+") is a mission for a flyby of the [Geminids](/wiki/Geminids "Geminids") meteor shower parent body [3200 Phaethon](/wiki/3200_Phaethon "3200 Phaethon"), as well as various minor bodies. Its launch is planned for fiscal year 2028.[[152]](#cite_note-158)

## Asteroid mining

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Asteroid mining")]

Main articles: [Asteroid mining](/wiki/Asteroid_mining "Asteroid mining") and [Colonization of the asteroids](/wiki/Colonization_of_the_asteroids "Colonization of the asteroids")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Artist_Concept_-_Astronaut_Performs_Tethering_Maneuvers_at_Asteroid.jpg/250px-Artist_Concept_-_Astronaut_Performs_Tethering_Maneuvers_at_Asteroid.jpg)](/wiki/File:Artist_Concept_-_Astronaut_Performs_Tethering_Maneuvers_at_Asteroid.jpg)

Artist's concept of a crewed mission to an asteroid

The concept of asteroid mining was proposed in the 1970s. Matt Anderson defines successful asteroid mining as "the development of a mining program that is both financially self-sustaining and profitable to its investors".[[153]](#cite_note-159) It has been suggested that asteroids might be used as a source of materials that may be rare or exhausted on Earth,[[154]](#cite_note-160) or materials for constructing [space habitats](/wiki/Space_habitat "Space habitat"). Materials that are heavy and expensive to launch from Earth may someday be mined from asteroids and used for [space manufacturing](/wiki/Space_manufacturing "Space manufacturing") and construction.[[155]](#cite_note-161)[[156]](#cite_note-162)

As [resource depletion](/wiki/Resource_depletion "Resource depletion") on Earth becomes more real, the idea of extracting valuable elements from asteroids and returning these to [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") for profit, or using space-based resources to build [solar-power satellites](/wiki/Space-based_solar_power "Space-based solar power") and [space habitats](/wiki/Space_habitats "Space habitats"),[[157]](#cite_note-163)[[158]](#cite_note-164) becomes more attractive. Hypothetically, water processed from ice could refuel orbiting [propellant depots](/wiki/Propellant_depot "Propellant depot").[[159]](#cite_note-165)[[160]](#cite_note-Kiss-166)

From the [astrobiological](/wiki/Astrobiology "Astrobiology") perspective, asteroid prospecting could provide scientific data for the search for extraterrestrial intelligence ([SETI](/wiki/SETI "SETI")). Some astrophysicists have suggested that if advanced extraterrestrial civilizations employed asteroid mining long ago, the hallmarks of these activities might be detectable.[[161]](#cite_note-167)[[162]](#cite_note-168)[[163]](#cite_note-169)

## Threats to Earth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Threats to Earth")]

See also: [List of Earth-crossing minor planets](/wiki/List_of_Earth-crossing_minor_planets "List of Earth-crossing minor planets")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/SmallAsteroidImpacts-Frequency-Bolide-20141114.jpg/250px-SmallAsteroidImpacts-Frequency-Bolide-20141114.jpg)](/wiki/File:SmallAsteroidImpacts-Frequency-Bolide-20141114.jpg)

Frequency of [bolides](/wiki/Bolide "Bolide"), small asteroids roughly 1 to 20 meters in diameter impacting Earth's atmosphere

There is increasing interest in identifying asteroids whose orbits cross [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s, and that could, given enough time, collide with Earth. The three most important groups of [near-Earth asteroids](/wiki/Near-Earth_asteroid "Near-Earth asteroid") are the [Apollos](/wiki/Apollo_asteroid "Apollo asteroid"), [Amors](/wiki/Amor_asteroid "Amor asteroid"), and [Atens](/wiki/Aten_asteroid "Aten asteroid").

The [near-Earth](/wiki/Near-Earth_object "Near-Earth object") asteroid [433 Eros](/wiki/433_Eros "433 Eros") had been discovered as long ago as 1898, and the 1930s brought a flurry of similar objects. In order of discovery, these were: [1221 Amor](/wiki/1221_Amor "1221 Amor"), [1862 Apollo](/wiki/1862_Apollo "1862 Apollo"), [2101 Adonis](/wiki/2101_Adonis "2101 Adonis"), and finally [69230 Hermes](/wiki/69230_Hermes "69230 Hermes"), which approached within 0.005 [AU](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit") of [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") in 1937. Astronomers began to realize the possibilities of Earth impact.

Two events in later decades increased the alarm: the increasing acceptance of the [Alvarez hypothesis](/wiki/Alvarez_hypothesis "Alvarez hypothesis") that an [impact event](/wiki/Impact_event "Impact event") resulted in the [Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction](/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event "Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event"), and the 1994 observation of [Comet Shoemaker-Levy 9](/wiki/Comet_Shoemaker-Levy_9 "Comet Shoemaker-Levy 9") crashing into [Jupiter](/wiki/Impact_events_on_Jupiter "Impact events on Jupiter"). The U.S. military also declassified the information that its [military satellites](/wiki/Military_satellite "Military satellite"), built to [detect nuclear explosions](/wiki/Detect_nuclear_explosions "Detect nuclear explosions"), had detected hundreds of upper-atmosphere impacts by objects ranging from one to ten meters across.

All of these considerations helped spur the launch of highly efficient surveys, consisting of charge-coupled device ([CCD](/wiki/Charge-coupled_device "Charge-coupled device")) cameras and computers directly connected to telescopes. As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit), it was estimated that 89% to 96% of near-Earth asteroids one kilometer or larger in diameter had been discovered.[[55]](#cite_note-nasa_neo-60) As of 29 October 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit), the LINEAR system alone had discovered 147,132 asteroids.[[164]](#cite_note-170) Among the surveys, 19,266 near-Earth asteroids have been discovered[[165]](#cite_note-171) including almost 900 more than 1 km (0.6 mi) in diameter.[[166]](#cite_note-172)

In June 2018, the [National Science and Technology Council](/wiki/National_Science_and_Technology_Council "National Science and Technology Council") warned that the United States is unprepared for an asteroid impact event, and has developed and released the "National Near-Earth Object Preparedness Strategy Action Plan" to better prepare.[[167]](#cite_note-GIZ-20180621-173)[[168]](#cite_note-ICARUS-220180522-174)[[169]](#cite_note-NYT-20180614-175) According to expert testimony in the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") in 2013, [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") would require at least five years of preparation before a mission to intercept an asteroid could be launched.[[170]](#cite_note-US-Congress-20130410-176)

### Asteroid deflection strategies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Asteroid deflection strategies")]

Main article: [Asteroid impact avoidance](/wiki/Asteroid_impact_avoidance "Asteroid impact avoidance")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Dart-poster3.jpg/250px-Dart-poster3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dart-poster3.jpg)

[Double Asteroid Redirection Test](/wiki/Double_Asteroid_Redirection_Test "Double Asteroid Redirection Test") in 2022 demonstrated that spacecraft impact is a viable option for [planetary defense](/wiki/Asteroid_impact_avoidance "Asteroid impact avoidance").

Various collision avoidance techniques have different trade-offs with respect to metrics such as overall performance, cost, failure risks, operations, and technology readiness.[[171]](#cite_note-177) There are various methods for changing the course of an asteroid/comet.[[172]](#cite_note-HallRoss-178) These can be differentiated by various types of attributes such as the type of mitigation (deflection or fragmentation), energy source (kinetic, electromagnetic, gravitational, solar/thermal, or nuclear), and approach strategy (interception,[[173]](#cite_note-179)[[174]](#cite_note-180) rendezvous, or remote station).

Strategies fall into two basic sets: fragmentation and delay.[[172]](#cite_note-HallRoss-178)[[175]](#cite_note-181) Fragmentation concentrates on rendering the impactor harmless by fragmenting it and scattering the fragments so that they miss the Earth or are small enough to burn up in the atmosphere. Delay exploits the fact that both the Earth and the impactor are in orbit. An impact occurs when both reach the same point in space at the same time, or more correctly when some point on Earth's surface intersects the impactor's orbit when the impactor arrives. Since the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") is approximately 12,750 km (7,920 mi) in diameter and moves at approx. 30 km/s (19 mi/s) in its orbit, it travels a distance of one planetary diameter in about 425 seconds, or slightly over seven minutes. Delaying, or advancing the impactor's arrival by times of this magnitude can, depending on the exact geometry of the impact, cause it to miss the Earth.[[176]](#cite_note-RossParkPorter-182)

"[Project Icarus](/wiki/1566_Icarus#Project_Icarus "1566 Icarus")" was one of the first projects designed in 1967 as a contingency plan in case of collision with [1566 Icarus](/wiki/1566_Icarus "1566 Icarus"). The plan relied on the new [Saturn V](/wiki/Saturn_V "Saturn V") rocket, which did not make its first flight until after the report had been completed. Six Saturn V rockets would be used, each launched at variable intervals from months to hours away from impact. Each rocket was to be fitted with a single 100-megaton [nuclear warhead](/wiki/Nuclear_warhead "Nuclear warhead") as well as a modified [Apollo Service Module](/wiki/Apollo_Service_Module "Apollo Service Module") and uncrewed [Apollo Command Module](/wiki/Apollo_Command_Module "Apollo Command Module") for guidance to the target. The warheads would be detonated 30 meters from the surface, deflecting or partially destroying the asteroid. Depending on the subsequent impacts on the course or the destruction of the asteroid, later missions would be modified or cancelled as needed. The "last-ditch" launch of the sixth rocket would be 18 hours prior to impact.[[177]](#cite_note-Portree-183)

## Fiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Fiction")]

Main article: [Asteroids in fiction](/wiki/Asteroids_in_fiction "Asteroids in fiction")

Asteroids and the asteroid belt are a staple of science fiction stories. Asteroids play several potential roles in science fiction: as places human beings might colonize, resources for extracting minerals, hazards encountered by spacecraft traveling between two other points, and as a threat to life on Earth or other inhabited planets, dwarf planets, and natural satellites by potential impact.[[178]](#cite_note-184)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Asteroid&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** Ceres is the largest asteroid and now classified as a [dwarf planet](/wiki/Dwarf_planet "Dwarf planet"). All other asteroids are now classified as [small Solar System bodies](/wiki/Small_Solar_System_body "Small Solar System body") along with comets, centaurs, and the smaller trans-Neptunian objects.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** In an oral presentation,[[10]](#cite_note-11) Clifford Cunningham presented his finding that the word was coined by Charles Burney, Jr., the son of a friend of Herschel.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** For example, the *Annual of Scientific Discovery*: "Professor J. Watson has been awarded by the Paris Academy of Sciences, the astronomical prize, Lalande foundation, for the discovery of eight new asteroids in one year. The planet [Lydia](/wiki/110_Lydia "110 Lydia") (No. 110), discovered by M. Borelly at the Marseilles Observatory [...] M. Borelly had previously discovered two planets bearing the numbers 91 and 99 in the system of asteroids revolving between Mars and Jupiter".[[13]](#cite_note-15)  
   The *Universal English Dictionary* (John Craig, 1869) lists the asteroids (and gives their pronunciations) up to [64 Angelina](/wiki/64_Angelina "64 Angelina"), along with the definition "one of the recently-discovered planets." At this time it was common to anglicize the spellings of the names, e.g. "Aglaia" for [47 Aglaja](/wiki/47_Aglaja "47 Aglaja") and "Atalanta" for [36 Atalante](/wiki/36_Atalante "36 Atalante").
4. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** For instance, a joint [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA")–[JPL](/wiki/JPL "JPL") public-outreach website states:
   > We include Trojans (bodies captured in Jupiter's 4th and 5th Lagrange points), Centaurs (bodies in orbit between Jupiter and Neptune), and trans-Neptunian objects (orbiting beyond Neptune) in our definition of "asteroid" as used on this site, even though they may more correctly be called "minor planets" instead of asteroids.[[17]](#cite_note-20)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-47)** Except for [Pluto](/wiki/Pluto "Pluto"), [99942 Apophis](/wiki/99942_Apophis "99942 Apophis") and, in the astrological community, for a few outer bodies such as [2060 Chiron](/wiki/2060_Chiron "2060 Chiron").
6. **[^](#cite_ref-78)** The definition in the 1995 paper (Beech and Steel) has been updated by a 2010 paper (Rubin and Grossman) and the discovery of 1 meter asteroids.

"Examination of the physical properties of the 41 largest and most massive main belt asteroids suggests that large asteroids without satellites are intact and their interiors have internal strength. This is consistent with results from the Dawn mission at both Vesta and Ceres. Ceres' volatile-rich composition also is a likely contributor to both the absence of satellites at Ceres and of Ceres meteorites at Earth. These results suggest that collisional disruption creating rubble pile structure is a necessary condition for formation of satellites around main belt asteroids."
